cards.back: Craniopharyngioma (most common hypothalamic tumor) and pituitary tumors; secondary tumors include those from breast, bronchus, and melanoma.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the first symptom of cranial diabetes insipidus in children?
cards.back: Polyuria (excessive urine output), typically 4-20 liters per day with dilute urine. In some cases, polydipsia (excessive thirst) occurs first in adults.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What complications can result from untreated cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Dehydration, weight loss, constipation, pyrexia (fever), and if ACTH is also deficient, these symptoms may be masked due to cortisol's role in water excretion. Polyuria may only manifest after cortisol replacement.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What compressive symptoms can occur with tumors causing cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Eye involvement (bitemporal hemianopia from optic chiasm compression) and cranial nerve palsies.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: How does concurrent ACTH deficiency affect the presentation of cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Cortisol is important for excretion of water load. With ACTH deficiency (cortisol deficiency), the polyuria may be masked, and diabetes insipidus may only become evident after cortisol replacement with hydrocortisone.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What basal investigations are performed for suspected cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Serum osmolality (elevated), urine osmolality (low), and urine specific gravity (<1.005). Dipstick urinalysis shows low specific gravity.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What does the water deprivation test demonstrate in cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: In normal individuals, fluid restriction maintains serum osmolality at the expense of urine osmolality. In diabetes insipidus, serum osmolality remains very high despite fluid restriction and only normalizes with exogenous (intranasal) ADH (desmopressin).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the differential diagnoses for cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Arginine vasopressin deficiency (cranial diabetes insipidus) vs. arginine vasopressin resistance (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, which may be congenital or acquired from renal disease).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What other conditions present with polyuria similar to diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Diabetes mellitus (urine specific gravity is high due to glucose), chronic renal failure, psychogenic polydipsia (compulsive water drinking), and electrolyte abnormalities (hypokalemia, hyperkalemia) including Conn syndrome or hyperparathyroidism.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the first-line treatment for cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Desmopressin (DDAVP - synthetic ADH) given as intranasal spray, oral tablets, or in acute settings via subcutaneous or intravenous injection. Intranasal is no longer commonly used; oral tablets are standard.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are alternative agents that were historically used for diabetes insipidus when desmopressin was unavailable?
cards.back: The "four Cs": chlorpropamide, carbamazepine, chlorothiazide, and proprietary ADH-enhancing drugs. With desmopressin now available, these are rarely used.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is syndrome of inappropriate ADH (SIADH)?
cards.back: Characterized by hyponatremia (low serum sodium) without edema, due to inappropriately excessive secretion of ADH in the presence of low serum osmolality.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What tumors can cause SIADH?
cards.back: Small cell lung carcinoma (most common), other bronchial carcinomas, duodenal/pancreatic carcinoma, gastric carcinoma, prostatic carcinoma, bronchial carcinoid, thymomas, and hematologic malignancies (lymphoma, leukemia, Hodgkin's disease).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What drugs can cause SIADH?
cards.back: Desmopressin (DDAVP), oxytocin, the "four Cs" (chlorpromidine, clofibrate, carbamazepine, chlorothiazide), antipsychotics (phenothiazines), tricyclic antidepressants, oncology drugs (vincristine, cyclophosphamide), and miscellaneous drugs (nicotine, NSAIDs).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What pulmonary conditions can cause SIADH?
cards.back: Tuberculosis (most common non-malignant cause), lung abscess, pneumonia, empyema, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What neurological conditions can cause SIADH?
cards.back: Head trauma, skull fractures, subdural hematoma, subarachnoid hemorrhage, cerebrovascular thrombosis, cerebral atrophy, acute meningitis, tuberculosis meningitis, pyogenic meningitis, Guillain-Barre syndrome, and lupus encephalitis.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What miscellaneous conditions can cause SIADH?
cards.back: Hypothyroidism and positive pressure ventilation (mechanical ventilation).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the clinical features of mild hyponatremia (serum sodium 130-135 mEq/L) in SIADH?
cards.back: Patients may be asymptomatic or present with anorexia, nausea, vomiting, lethargy, and weight gain.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the clinical features of severe acute hyponatremia (<120 mEq/L) in SIADH?
cards.back: Worsening lethargy, weight gain, confusion, convulsions, coma, and death if untreated. Importantly, edema and hypertension are rare in SIADH.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: Why is the rate of sodium decline important in SIADH?
cards.back: Rapid decline in serum sodium causes more severe symptoms than gradual decline at the same final sodium level.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the diagnostic criteria for SIADH?
cards.back: Serum sodium <130 mEq/L, low serum osmolality with elevated urine osmolality, urine sodium elevated (>40 mEq/L), and absence of hypotension, edema, or dehydration. Elevated plasma ADH (if measurable) confirms diagnosis.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What biochemical abnormalities are seen in SIADH?
cards.back: Low serum urea, creatinine, and uric acid; low albumin; low bicarbonate and potassium; low serum osmolality; and elevated urine osmolality and urine sodium.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the three types of hyponatremia based on volume status?
cards.back: Type 1 (hypervolemic): cardiac failure, liver disease, renal disease. Type 2 (hypovolemic): dehydration from diarrhea or vomiting. Type 3 (euvolemic): SIADH (no edema, no hypertension).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What other causes of low serum sodium must be considered besides SIADH?
cards.back: Primary polydipsia, hypotension, pseudo-hyponatremia (from hyperlipidemia or hyperproteinemia causing laboratory artifact), and hyperglycemia.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the management goal for SIADH?
cards.back: Increase serum sodium toward normal (>135 mEq/L) slowly and identify the underlying cause.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the fluid restriction for mild asymptomatic SIADH?
cards.back: Restrict fluid to approximately 500 mL per day.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the treatment for severe symptomatic SIADH?
cards.back: Hypertonic saline (3-5% saline) given carefully, with other agents like tolvaptan (vasopressin antagonist), lithium, glucocorticoids, and demeclocycline available as alternatives.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the critical safety consideration when correcting hyponatremia in SIADH?
cards.back: Increase serum sodium slowly—no more than 12 mEq/L per day. Rapid correction can cause central pontine myelinolysis (CPM), a devastating neurological condition characterized by quadriplegia, ataxia, and abnormal eye movements with high mortality.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is central pontine myelinolysis (CPM)?
cards.back: A serious neurological complication of too-rapid sodium correction in hyponatremia, characterized by quadriplegia, ataxia, extraocular muscle weakness, and abnormal ocular movements, with very high mortality.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: Which specific zone of the adrenal cortex is responsible for the production of mineralocorticoids like aldosterone?
cards.back: Zona glomerulosa

cards.front: What is the primary androgen produced by the adrenal reticularis?
cards.back: Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA)

cards.front: In primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease), why does hyperpigmentation occur?
cards.back: Increased ACTH production is accompanied by $\beta$-MSH (melanocyte-stimulating hormone) production.

cards.front: Which clinical sign in the nails is specifically associated with Addison's disease?
cards.back: Brown nails

cards.front: What cardiovascular finding is typically seen on a chest X-ray of a patient with Addison's disease?
cards.back: A small heart

cards.front: In the context of adrenal hypofunction, what is the 'Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome'?
cards.back: Adrenal hemorrhage resulting from meningococcal septicemia, typically in children.

cards.front: Why must one be cautious when using Prednisone as maintenance therapy for adrenal insufficiency compared to Hydrocortisone?
cards.back: Prednisone use prevents the accurate measurement of the serum cortisol response.

cards.front: What is the gold standard provocative test used to confirm a diagnosis of hypocortisolism?
cards.back: Synacthen (ACTH) stimulation test

cards.front: During a short Synacthen test, a normal individual's cortisol should rise to at least what level?
cards.back: $550\ nmol/L$

cards.front: What is the physiological rationale for the 'long' (4-day) Synacthen test?
cards.back: Continual ACTH stimulation will eventually 'kickstart' the adrenal glands in secondary (pituitary) insufficiency, but not in primary disease.

cards.front: What is the immediate emergency treatment for an Addisonian crisis?
cards.back: IV fluids (Normal or Dextrose Saline) and $100\ mg$ of Hydrocortisone IV stat.

cards.front: How is 'Cushing's disease' specifically defined compared to 'Cushing's syndrome'?
cards.back: It is hypercortisolism caused specifically by a pituitary microadenoma secreting excess ACTH.

cards.front: Which malignancy is the most common cause of ectopic ACTH secretion leading to Cushing's syndrome?
cards.back: Small cell carcinoma of the bronchus

cards.front: In Cushing's syndrome, what width must abdominal striae generally exceed to be considered 'vivid' or 'purple' striae?
What is the characteristic electrolyte abnormality found in severe Cushing's syndrome or ectopic ACTH cases?
cards.back: Hypokalemic alkalosis

cards.front: What diagnostic test is used to establish the loss of diurnal rhythm in cortisol secretion?
cards.back: Midnight serum or salivary cortisol measurement

cards.front: In a high-dose ($8\ mg$) Dexamethasone suppression test, what percentage of cortisol suppression suggests a pituitary source (Cushing's disease)?
cards.back: Greater than $68\%$ suppression

cards.front: What is the purpose of Inferior Petrosal Sinus Sampling (IPSS) in the workup of Cushing's syndrome?
cards.back: To distinguish between a pituitary (central) source and an ectopic (peripheral) source of ACTH.

cards.front: What is Nelson's syndrome?
cards.back: The autonomous enlargement of a pituitary tumor following bilateral adrenalectomy due to the loss of cortisol feedback.

cards.front: Which zone of the adrenal cortex is involved in primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn's syndrome)?
cards.back: Zona glomerulosa

cards.front: Why is edema typically absent in patients with primary hyperaldosteronism?
cards.back: The 'escape phenomenon' triggered by the release of atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP).

cards.front: In the investigation of hypertension and hypokalemia, what Aldosterone-Renin Ratio (ARR) value suggests primary hyperaldosteronism?
cards.back: Equal to or greater than $50$ (when using renin activity) or $90$ (when using concentration).

cards.front: What are 'paragangliomas'?
cards.back: Catecholamine-secreting tumors arising from chromaffin cells in the sympathetic ganglia outside the adrenal medulla.

cards.front: List the 'Five Ps' associated with the clinical presentation of Pheochromocytoma.
cards.back: Pressure (BP), Pain (Headache), Perspiration, Palpitations, and Pallor.

cards.front: Why might a patient with an adrenaline-secreting pheochromocytoma paradoxically present with postural hypotension?
cards.back: Decreased plasma volume and a blunted sympathetic response.

cards.front: What is the gold standard biochemical test for diagnosing Pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: $24$-hour urine metanephrines

cards.front: In the preoperative management of Pheochromocytoma, what is the critical sequence of adrenergic blockade?
cards.back: Alpha-adrenergic blockade must be established before starting beta-blockers.

cards.front: What is the WHO diagnostic threshold for fasting plasma glucose in Diabetes Mellitus?
cards.back: Equal to or greater than $7.0\ mmol/L$

cards.front: According to the ADA, what HbA1c level is diagnostic for Diabetes Mellitus?
cards.back: Equal to or greater than $6.5\%$

cards.front: How is Impaired Glucose Tolerance (IGT) defined based on a $2$-hour post-glucose load test?
cards.back: Plasma glucose between $7.8\ mmol/L$ and $11.0\ mmol/L$.

cards.front: What is the primary pathogenic distinction between Type 1 and Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus?
cards.back: Type 1 is characterized by absolute insulin deficiency (autoimmune), while Type 2 involves insulin resistance and relative deficiency.

cards.front: What biochemical test can distinguish Type 1 from Type 2 Diabetes by measuring endogenous insulin production?
cards.back: C-peptide (basal or glucagon-stimulated)

cards.front: What does the ADA category of 'increased risk for diabetes' (pre-diabetes) define for HbA1c?
What are the three specific microvascular complications of Diabetes Mellitus?
cards.back: Retinopathy, nephropathy, and neuropathy.

cards.front: The characteristic histological lesion of diabetic nephropathy is known as _____.
cards.back: Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules (glomerulosclerosis)

cards.front: How is 'microalbuminuria' (incipient nephropathy) defined in a $24$-hour urine collection?
Which class of antihypertensive drugs is first-line for treating diabetic nephropathy due to its renoprotective effects?
cards.back: ACE inhibitors (or ARBs)

cards.front: At what GFR level is Metformin typically contraindicated in diabetic patients?
cards.back: Less than $30\ ml/min$ (or sometimes stopped at $45\ ml/min$ depending on local guidelines).

cards.front: What ocular finding characterizes the transition from non-proliferative to proliferative diabetic retinopathy?
cards.back: New vessel formation (neovascularization) at the disc or elsewhere.

cards.front: What is the commonest symptom of autonomic neuropathy in the gastrointestinal tract for diabetic patients?
cards.back: Gastroparesis (manifesting as bloating, nausea, or vomiting).

cards.front: Which sensory modality is typically the first to be lost in diabetic peripheral neuropathy?
cards.back: Vibration sense

cards.front: What defines a 'macro adenoma' of the pituitary gland?
cards.back: A tumor with a diameter greater than $10\ mm$.

cards.front: What is the visual field defect typically caused by a pituitary tumor pressing on the optic chiasm?
cards.back: Bitemporal hemianopia

cards.front: What facial feature describes the forward protrusion of the lower jaw in acromegaly?
cards.back: Prognathism

cards.front: Which screening test is most sensitive for diagnosing acromegaly due to its stable levels throughout the day?
cards.back: Serum IGF-1 (Insulin-like Growth Factor 1)

cards.front: How is the Oral Glucose Tolerance Test (OGTT) used to diagnose acromegaly?
cards.back: A failure of growth hormone (GH) to suppress to basal levels following a glucose load.

cards.front: What is the first-line treatment for most functioning pituitary tumors, except for prolactinomas?
cards.back: Transphenoidal surgery

cards.front: What is the medical treatment of choice for a prolactinoma?
cards.back: Dopamine agonists (e.g., Bromocriptine or Cabergoline).

cards.front: Define 'Sheehan's syndrome'.
cards.back: Postpartum necrosis of the pituitary gland following severe obstetric hemorrhage and hypotension.

cards.front: What is the usual sequence of hormone loss in progressive pan-hypopituitarism?
cards.back: Growth Hormone $\rightarrow$ Prolactin $\rightarrow$ Gonadotropins $\rightarrow$ TSH $\rightarrow$ ACTH.

cards.front: In the management of pan-hypopituitarism, why must Hydrocortisone be replaced before Levothyroxine?
cards.back: Thyroxine can accelerate cortisol degradation and precipitate an adrenal crisis in an untreated hypo-adrenal patient.

cards.front: What is the hallmark clinical presentation of cranial diabetes insipidus (Arginine Vasopressin Deficiency)?
cards.back: Polyuria ($4$ to $20\ L/day$) and polydipsia with dilute urine.

cards.front: How does the Water Deprivation Test distinguish between cranial and nephrogenic diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Urine osmolality increases after exogenous desmopressin (DDAVP) in cranial DI, but fails to increase in nephrogenic DI.

cards.front: What is the primary treatment for Arginine Vasopressin Deficiency (cranial DI)?
cards.back: Desmopressin (DDAVP)

cards.front: SIADH is characterized by what type of hyponatremia?
cards.back: Euvolemic hyponatremia

cards.front: What is the most dangerous complication of correcting chronic hyponatremia too rapidly?
cards.back: Central Pontine Myelinolysis

cards.front: What is the recommended maximum rate of sodium correction in SIADH over 24 hours?
cards.back: $12\ mmol/L$

cards.front: How is the 'corrected calcium' calculated for patients with low albumin?
cards.back: $Corrected\ Ca^{2+} = Measured\ Ca^{2+} + [0.02 \times (40 - Albumin)]$

cards.front: List the classic quartet of symptoms associated with primary hyperparathyroidism.
cards.back: Bones (osteitis fibrosa cystica), Stones (renal calculi), Abdominal groans (constipation/ulcers), and Psychic moans (depression).

cards.front: What radiologic appearance of the skull is characteristic of hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Salt and pepper (or pepper pot) appearance.

cards.front: What is 'osteitis fibrosa cystica'?
cards.back: Bone cysts and subperiosteal resorption caused by excessive PTH activity.

cards.front: Define 'Secondary Hyperparathyroidism'.
cards.back: Compensatory PTH elevation in response to chronic hypocalcemia (e.g., in renal failure or Vitamin D deficiency).

cards.front: Define 'Tertiary Hyperparathyroidism'.
cards.back: Autonomous PTH secretion following prolonged secondary hyperparathyroidism, leading to hypercalcemia.

cards.front: What imaging study is most specific for localizing a parathyroid adenoma?
cards.back: Sestamibi scan

cards.front: What ECG change is typically seen in patients with hypercalcemia?
cards.back: Shortening of the QT interval.

cards.front: What is 'Chvostek’s sign'?
cards.back: Tapping the facial nerve induces facial muscle contraction, indicating latent tetany/hypocalcemia.

cards.front: What is 'Trousseau’s sign'?
cards.back: Inducing carpal spasm by inflating a blood pressure cuff above systolic pressure for three minutes.

cards.front: What is 'Pseudo-hypoparathyroidism'?
cards.back: A genetic condition characterized by end-organ resistance to PTH, resulting in high PTH levels but low serum calcium.

cards.front: Which physical finding in the hand is classic for Albright’s hereditary osteodystrophy (Pseudo-hypoparathyroidism)?
cards.back: A short fourth metacarpal (manifests as a 'missing' knuckle when making a fist).

cards.front: What ECG change is characteristic of hypocalcemia?
cards.back: Prolongation of the corrected QT (QTc) interval.

cards.front: What is the commonest cause of acquired hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Accidental surgical removal or damage during thyroidectomy.

cards.front: In patients with diabetic neuropathy, what is the 'monofilament' test used to assess?
cards.back: Pressure perception/touch sensation to identify risk for foot ulceration.

cards.front: Which clinical scenario describes 'hyperglycemic crisis' in Type 2 Diabetes?
cards.back: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS), previously known as HONK.

cards.front: What is the definitive treatment for symptomatic primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Surgical removal of the parathyroid adenoma.

cards.front: Which biochemical markers are elevated in the diagnosis of SIADH?
cards.back: Urine sodium and urine osmolality (in the presence of low serum osmolality).

cards.front: A patient with $24$-hour urine albumin of $450\ mg$ has reached which stage of diabetic nephropathy?
cards.back: Stage 4 (Established or Overt Nephropathy).

cards.front: Which 'P' of the pheochromocytoma clinical presentation involves intense headache?
cards.back: Pain

cards.front: What is the medical management for 'painful' diabetic neuropathy?
cards.back: Tricyclic antidepressants (Amitriptyline), anticonvulsants (Gabapentin/Pregabalin), or SNRIs (Duloxetine).

cards.front: What are the three hormones involved in calcium homeostasis?
cards.back: PTH (parathyroid hormone), vitamin D, and calcitonin

cards.front: What are the three main organs involved in calcium homeostasis?
cards.back: Bone, gut (GI tract), and kidneys

cards.front: Describe the action of PTH on calcium and phosphate
cards.back: PTH acts on bone and kidney, resulting in increased calcium and decreased phosphate

cards.front: Describe the action of vitamin D on calcium and phosphate
cards.back: Vitamin D acts on bone and GI tract, increasing both calcium and phosphate

cards.front: What is the net effect of calcitonin on serum calcium and phosphate?
cards.back: Calcitonin causes decreased calcium and decreased phosphate

cards.front: Which form of calcium is physiologically important?
cards.back: Free or ionized calcium (not bound to albumin)

cards.front: How is corrected serum calcium calculated?
cards.back: (40 - serum albumin) × 0.02 + measured serum calcium

cards.front: What defines primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Autonomous/excessive PTH secretion with elevated calcium and elevated PTH

cards.front: What are the causes of primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Adenoma (solitary or multiple), hyperplasia, rarely carcinoma. May be part of MEN 1 or MEN 2

cards.front: What defines secondary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Compensatory increase in PTH due to low calcium. Causes: chronic renal disease, malabsorption, rickets

cards.front: What is tertiary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Autonomous PTH secretion developing after prolonged secondary hyperparathyroidism. Elevated PTH with elevated calcium

cards.front: What is ectopic hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: PTH-related protein secretion from malignancy. High calcium with low or absent measurable PTH

cards.front: What are the renal manifestations of primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Nephrolithiasis, nephrocalcinosis, hypertension, polyuria, polydipsia, renal tubular acidosis, Fanconi syndrome

cards.front: What is the relationship between nephrocalcinosis and hypercalcemia?
cards.back: Hypercalcemia causes inability to concentrate urine (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus) and renal tubular dysfunction

cards.front: What are the bone manifestations of primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Osteitis fibrosa cystica, brown tumors, pathological fractures, subperiosteal reabsorption

cards.front: Which bones are classically affected by osteitis fibrosa cystica?
cards.back: Mandible, humerus, femur, clavicle. Subperiosteal reabsorption typically on right middle finger (radial aspect)

cards.front: What GI manifestations can hypercalcemia cause?
cards.back: Anorexia, nausea, weight loss, dyspepsia, peptic ulcer, abdominal colic, obstipation (obstinate constipation), pancreatitis

cards.front: What is the relationship between hypercalcemia and pancreatitis?
cards.back: Bidirectional: pancreatitis causes low calcium, while hyperparathyroidism can cause pancreatitis

cards.front: What mental manifestations can hypercalcemia cause?
cards.back: Anxiety, irritability, depression - often first to resolve after successful parathyroidectomy

cards.front: What ophthalmologic findings occur in hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Band keratopathy (corneal calcium deposits), pseudogout/chondrocalcinosis in joints

cards.front: What is acute hypercalcemia?
cards.back: Medical emergency: serum calcium ≥3.5-3.7 mmol/L with shock-like state, oliguria, dehydration, pyrexia, coma

cards.front: What are key laboratory findings in primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Elevated serum calcium, low phosphate, increased urinary calcium, elevated PTH, elevated alkaline phosphatase

cards.front: What acid-base abnormality occurs in primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Hypochloremic alkalosis (high bicarb, low chloride)

cards.front: What radiological features are seen in primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Subperiosteal reabsorption, salt-and-pepper skull appearance, cysts in bones, brown tumors, vertebral sclerosis

cards.front: How does PTH affect cortical vs cancellous bone in hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Cortical bone (distal radius) affected MORE than cancellous bone (spine)

cards.front: What is sestamibi scan used for?
cards.back: Radionuclide imaging to localize parathyroid adenomas

cards.front: What ECG finding is seen in hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Shortened corrected QT interval (normal 0.35-0.40 seconds)

cards.front: What intraoperative test confirms successful parathyroidectomy?
cards.back: Dramatic reduction in PTH levels after adenoma resection

cards.front: What is the treatment of choice for primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Surgical treatment with PTH monitoring. For adenoma: resect adenoma. For hyperplasia: remove 3.5 glands

cards.front: What medical therapy is used for primary hyperparathyroidism when surgery not possible?
cards.back: Calcimimetic agents (cinacalcet) targeting calcium sensing receptors to suppress PTH

cards.front: How is secondary hyperparathyroidism treated?
cards.back: If malabsorption: vitamin D. If renal disease: vitamin D or alpha-calcidiol

cards.front: What is the acute management of severe hypercalcemia?
cards.back: Rehydration with normal saline, bisphosphonates (alendronate or ibandronate), steroids for myeloma

cards.front: How is hypoparathyroidism defined?
cards.back: Inadequate PTH secretion or resistance to PTH, causing low calcium and high phosphate

cards.front: What are the causes of hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Hereditary (idiopathic, polyglandular autoimmune, Albright syndrome), acquired (surgical, radiation), infiltration (TB, amyloid, sarcoid), hypermagnesemia

cards.front: What are the neuromuscular manifestations of hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Tetany, paresthesias, muscle cramps, Trousseau sign (carpopedal spasm), Chvostek sign (facial twitch), brisk reflexes, convulsions

cards.front: What is Trousseau sign?
cards.back: Blood pressure cuff inflated above systolic for 3 minutes causes carpopedal spasm - indicates latent tetany

cards.front: What is Chvostek sign and its significance?
cards.back: Tapping facial nerve over parotid induces facial contraction. Present in ~5% of normal population so NOT specific

cards.front: What CNS complications occur in hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Cataracts, benign intracranial hypertension (bilateral papilledema), epilepsy, basal ganglia calcification

cards.front: What is FAHR syndrome?
cards.back: Familial Hypoparathyroidism with basal ganglia calcification and Extrapyramidal symptoms

cards.front: What dermatological findings are seen in hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Dry scaly skin, eczema, alopecia, brittle deformed nails prone to paronychia/candida, malformed teeth if early onset

cards.front: What are the laboratory findings in hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Low serum calcium, high phosphate, low/absent PTH, decreased urinary calcium and phosphate, possible low magnesium

cards.front: What ECG finding is seen in hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Prolonged corrected QT interval (opposite of hyperparathyroidism)

cards.front: How is transient hypoparathyroidism treated?
cards.back: Oral calcium carbonate, or intravenous calcium gluconate if severe

cards.front: How is chronic hypoparathyroidism treated?
cards.back: Vitamin D analogs (1-alpha, alpha-calcidiol), calcitriol, teriparatide (PTH) if available. Treat hypomagnesemia

cards.front: What is the prognosis of hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Post-operative transient: good. Early detection: good. Late detection: worse

cards.front: What is pseudohypoparathyroidism (PHP)?
cards.back: Normal/hypoplastic parathyroid glands but target organs (kidney, bone) resist PTH. Biochemistry of hypoparathyroidism

cards.front: What causes pseudohypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Deficiency of alpha subunit of G protein (Gα), causing end-organ PTH resistance

cards.front: What is Albright hereditary osteodystrophy?
cards.back: PHP phenotype: short stature, skeletal abnormalities, round face with frontal bossing, short metacarpals/phalanges

cards.front: What skeletal features characterize PHP?
cards.back: Short 4th and 5th metacarpals, short phalanges, short stubby hands, heterotopic ossifications, basal ganglia calcification

cards.front: What neurological features can PHP have?
cards.back: Mental deficiency, epilepsy, tetany, basal ganglia calcification (like hypoparathyroidism)

cards.front: How is short 4th metacarpal detected clinically?
cards.back: Patient makes a fist - normally all metacarpal heads level. In PHP, 4th metacarpal pushed down and not visible

cards.front: What laboratory findings distinguish PHP from hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Both have low calcium and high phosphate, BUT PHP has elevated PTH (not low) - the key difference

cards.front: What is the Ellsworth-Howard test?
cards.back: Measures urinary cAMP and phosphate to differentiate hypoparathyroidism from PHP and its subtypes

atoms.source: Diagnosis & Classification of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1
atoms.description:
Clinical stages of glycemia: normal (<6.1 mmol/L), impaired glucose tolerance (6.1-11.1 mmol/L at 2h), 
diabetes (≥11.1 mmol/L at 2h or ≥7 fasting)
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Diagnostic criteria for diabetes using fasting plasma glucose

atoms.description:
Type 1 presents with rapid DKA; Type 2 with slow onset, often overweight; LADA hybrid phenotype 
with autoimmune features
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Compare age of onset and body composition between type 1 and type 2

atoms.description:
Type 1 diabetes acute crisis: rapidly progresses to diabetic ketoacidosis with acidosis, 
ketonuria, hyperglycemia
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Acute crisis presentation for untreated type 1 vs type 2 diabetes

atoms.description:
Type 2 diabetes acute crisis: hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state without significant ketosis, 
severe hyperglycemia, dehydration

Hypoglycemia: adrenergic symptoms (tremor, palpitations, sweating, anxiety) and 
neuroglycopenic (confusion, headache, coma)

TSH suppressed (first), T4 elevated, T3 elevated; Graves disease with anti-TSH receptor antibody 
and eye signs
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Thyroid hormone measurement pattern in thyrotoxicosis

atoms.description:
Primary hypothyroidism: TSH elevated, T4 low; Secondary: TSH low, T4 low; 
Central: TSH low with inadequate T4 response
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Diagnosis of primary vs secondary hypothyroidism

atoms.source: Disorders of the Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1
atoms.description:
Isolated hypopituitarism: single hormone deficiency (ACTH, TSH, gonadotropins, GH); 
Pan-hypopituitarism: multiple deficiencies
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Difference between isolated and pan-hypopituitarism

atoms.description:
Excess GH and IGF-1 causing coarse features, prognathism, organomegaly, hypertension, glucose intolerance; 
fails to suppress on OGTT
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Gold standard provocative test for acromegaly - OGTT

atoms.description:
Prolactin >4× upper limit normal with galactorrhea, amenorrhea, infertility, decreased libido; 
MRI identifies macroadenoma

Addison disease: elevated ACTH, low cortisol, hyperpigmentation, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia,
